speaker: interviewee
text: --immigrated to this country.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is your husband going to come back, though?

speaker: interviewee
text: He going to later, a little later.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Leslie-- this is the interview with Rose Minsky. I am Leslie Troper, the interviewer. We're in Brooklyn, New York on December 7, 1994.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came into that gestapo. And we said the same story. And the gestapo man says to us like this, don't be afraid. If you're Jewish, just admit it. We're going to put you-- take you to your sisters, where you belong. We find a lot of Jewish people here, escapees, so don't be afraid to admit if you're Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had to play-- I had to become right away an actress and be calm, not to show him that I'm afraid. And I said to him, unless you want to force me to admit, I cannot admit that I'm Jewish when I'm Catholic from-- from-- from generations. My father was Catholic, and my grandfather was Catholic, and my great grandfather. So all of a sudden, I should become Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had the nerve to tell him this. He calls in another one, a younger one. The younger are worse than the old one, also with the armband and the same thing. Nothing bad will happen to you. You're Jewish, we'll take you to a camp. Like I wouldn't know what a  camp is, like I wouldn't know, after Majdanek and after being almost two years in Auschwitz. Like I wouldn't know. He tells me that they are going to take me. And I said again the same thing. I said I cannot admit that I'm Jewish when I'm not Jewish. I'm from generations, generations, and generations of Catholics. And I didn't know one prayer in Catholic, in Polish. And if he would raise my arm, my sleeve, he would see right away that I'm Jewish. Because the Jewish number-- you see, it's still visible-- the Jewish number got a triangle. And the triangle was for Jews. See, I couldn't get out everything. I got it out as an emergency. I was afraid that they were going to see this. And, well, interrogated me, interrogated me. And, finally, I see the young man sit down near a typewriter, starts typing. Ask me questions, what's your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right away, Rosalia Warshawska, a Polish name. Where you live in Warsaw? I gave him the section that the Poles live, not the Jewish section. What's your father's name? Josef Warshawska, Polish name. Mother's name? Again, I made up names right away, on the spot. And whatever I say, he types.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I figure to myself, my God, I'm going to get a document that people in the ghetto would give millions of dollars. To have a piece of paper, with a Polish name. With a document like this, and I got with a couple lies. It was towards the end, I think. I still cannot figure out if they were already losing the war, and he didn't care. Or-- or-- or he believed me, one or the other. Anyway, he typed-- typed me up a paper, put his seal on it, that Hitler's seal, with that swastika. And I have a paper in my hand, and the other one, too, my friend. Now he took us-- it was already getting towards the evening. He took us to sleep over somewhere, to a German woman with six kids, also without a husband. The husband must have been in the war. Gave us a room over there. And in the morning, he said-- this was the worst part-- in the morning, they're going to come to pick us up for more interrogation. And this was the worst thing that I was afraid. For being interrogated, and it shouldn't come out that we're Jewish. But we had no choice. All of a sudden, the German woman, on the order of the police, of the gestapo, gives us a separate room, with a clean bed, a warm blanket. And we were pinching each other, if it's true. From Auschwitz right in a bed, and brings us-- and gives us-- calls us in for warm soup with bread. I said, this must be a dream. We're dreaming. It's not true. But still, we couldn't close our eyes from excitement, and we couldn't-- from worrying what's going to happen in the-- in the morning. But God helped. During the night, we hear all the sirens in that little-- in that town, blowing away. And the radio announces-- I understand German-- announces that the Russian army is very near. We are surrounded. And the whole-- the whole town is being evacuated because it was an industrial town, doing-- with a lot of factories. Every factory, every bridge will be blown up by the SS. It will be dynamite. They don't leave for the Russians a thing that can be useful.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the whole population got to come to the-- you know, to the trains, where the train is going away. And we'd be shipped-- we'd be shipped to deep Germany. And I say to her, what shall we do. Shall we hide? So we might be-- after being almost on the way of getting-- being free, people, if you hide, you might be blown up by the SS. They warned people not to hide, because everything will be blown up. Not to stay back, but go with the transport. They said on every state train station, on every station there will be Red Cross people giving us hot soup and coffee and bread, nourishment. We'd be sleeping in the evening in movies and theaters and schools.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were thinking and thinking. I said to her, you know something, we cannot risk-- we are so far, we went so far-- to hide and to find our dead. We're going to mix with the German population. And we're going to be refugees together with them. We're going to be running away from the Russians to deep Germany. Because otherwise, we'll be blown up here. And that's what we did. They gave us food on the way. They gave us-- in one of the places, until they brought us to Waldenburg. Today, this belongs to the Poles, in upper Silesia. This is Walbrzych. Today, the name is Walbrzych in Polish. This is Waldenburg. It was a German town. And we slept over there in one of the theaters. And straw-- they prepared the Red Cross, prepared straws-- straw on the floor. And we slept. Laying on the floor-- there were two women, full theater with people. Men and children, everybody runs away from the Russians. And they're helping them on the way, the Red Cross. Laying next to us, there were in the nighttime two women. And I overheard them talking. That in the morning, soon it's going to get daylight. They're going to leave the place. And they're going to go to the [GERMAN] looking for work. [GERMAN] means employment office. To look for work. And I said, you know, we're going to follow them. And we're going to go to that [GERMAN]. And we're going to look-- because I'm not afraid no more. I've got a document who I am. And you've got a document. We're Polish girls. They can't kill Polish girls, they won't kill. Unless we're Jewish. I said, we're going to mix together with them. And whatever work they give me, I'm going to accept, to live through the war. We followed them, and we stood on line, and we told them the same story, that we were working in this and that town. And the Russians are there already. And we didn't want to be with the Russians. We're looking for work. We got work. I got a job. I was paid 120 marks a week. In Waldenburg, there was a big house. They call it-- it's like a hotel. It was like a hotel for people-- for-- the high ranking people were there. I didn't know they sent me to a dangerous place again. But I had to accept. To be a chambermaid in a hotel, and the other one too, my friend. To make the beds, to clean, to help out a little bit in the kitchen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they gave me a letter from the unemployment office to show that they need people to work in this place. We went there. And the woman in charge of that-- of that hotel was a [NON-ENGLISH]. That was-- she was from Polish mix, Polish and German parents. And she heard me-- that I'm a Polish girl, she was very happy with me. She said, I didn't have a chance to speak Polish in so many years. Now I'm going to refresh. She will refresh her Polish. She hired me right away. She liked me, that woman. But it was a dangerous place. It was a lot of SS men there. Instead of being on the front and fight for Germany, they hide over there. They had it good. They had that cellar, a basement with food that could last them 10 more years over there. Food was over the head to eat over there. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And she saw that I am a good worker, because I didn't want to be recognized. So I put all my-- all my being into work. Work, work. She said, take a rest sometimes. Don't work so hard. Take a rest, she said to me. I overheard them talking, the German SS, that the best pleasure is to go to a big town-- Katowice, it's a town in Poland-- and to cut by the religious Jews, the beards and the side locks. I made believe I don't understand. They wanted to talk to me. I said [GERMAN]. I don't understand. I made believe I'm stupid. They want to go with me to the movies. They want to go to a dance with me, the officers. I said I'm not going nowhere, made believe that I'm a religious Christian, and I don't make business with no men. They want to-- they want to be friendly with me, because they thought I'm really Polish. I said I won't talk to them, I won't make business with nobody. I said I got a sweetheart at home, and as soon as I come, I get married, to get out of all troubles. Matter of fact, when somebody wanted to start up with me, I heard the other said in German to him, leave her alone, she's stupid. She's got to have a boyfriend somewhere. She won't go with you. [LAUGHS] I had to play like this. Otherwise, I wouldn't survive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, what I notice is a couple-- not far away from that place was a camp with Jewish men. They were wearing those-- they were wearing those striped uniforms. And I even managed that the leftovers, instead of throwing away, I spoke to the real German girls that were working there, to bring out for the Jewish men something to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: You weren't afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was afraid to do it, but I told them. They did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were not afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: They could do it, whatever. They German girls. They wouldn't hit them for doing it. And this was a civilian woman. She was not a military person. I even-- and one send us a note that we somehow will pull through. But they were smokers. If I can get cigarette butts, to take off from the tables and throw them down from the window. I was afraid to do it, but I did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a labor camp or a concentration camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a branch from Gross-- from a big camp, Gross-Rosen. And the men were working in a factory there. I think in a [NON-ENGLISH] factory, they were working, for the Germans. Still, they helped me a lot, these men. They had in their camp a tailoring shop. They were doing tailoring work. And I wrote a note to them. Because some of the boys came in to do work with a gendarme to watch them. Like say they have to do cleaning the basements or doing some electrical work, so they choose electricians they took. And they came in to do the work. I slipped a note to these boys. We are escapees from Auschwitz, from Birkenau. And we have numbers on our arms. In case if they going to roll our sleeve up, they see who we are, they're going to kill us. Maybe they have a doctor there to help us to get rid of the number. And they did. Next day came another group to do work. They dropped me a little package. I had there my room. I could go to the room and lock myself and do for myself what I had to do. In that package was acid, a little bottle of acid, and like a thing like a white pencil, that people burn out warts from the-- they want to get rid of warts from-- was instructions from a doctor to dip-- I should dip that pencil in that acid and rub on the number. And the number will become dry and will fall off. But I couldn't wait until the number will become dry. It should take a couple of days, because my boss-- the Fraulein Opalla was her name. I still remember her name, Fraulein Opalla. My boss brought a letter that it's required as I work and help in the kitchen, I should go through a doctor's check-up, if I am healthy. And here, I got the number, and I'm going to undress by the doctor. He'll see who I am. And I was terribly afraid of this.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I couldn't wait. I did what the doctor said, and this number became even more visible. Instead of black letters, the letters became white. And the skin became like-- like wood here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I ripped it off. And I go to the doctor. I bandaged my arm. And I'm going to tell him that I burned myself on the stove. The blood started gushing. I ripped off myself that thing. And I had terrible pain. And the other wanted the same thing. We both did the job. We locked ourselves and we did the job. Luckily that she went to another doctor, and I went to another. If we would go to the same doctor, he would see that something is wrong that we'd both got the same problem. I went to the doctor. After I went to the doctor, I realized that I made all the pain for nothing. He give us a note, then he didn't even care.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, my nerves they was terrible. And he only asked me, what have you got here. Why is it bandaged?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said I burned myself on the stove. And he said, for  a burn, it's better, when you come home, I should leave it open. It's going to dry better. It's going to heal better. That was his advice. I shouldn't even go through with this. Well, I could-- I could bandage my arm. But I shouldn't do pain to myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this-- I pulled through this too. And that's how I worked there. It was a very dangerous place to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to be a babysitter to one officer, an SS man, to his two children, because he went with his wife to the movies. I had to-- they put out the boots outside the door, I had to shine them. I had to  shine their boots. I did everything because I-- this was the only place where I could live through until the end. And one day, I had the biggest satisfaction of my life. Then the Fraulein Opalla, my boss, called me in and said to me, we heard already the shots are coming nearer and nearer. She said, in a couple of hours, the Russians will be here. They're going to think I am a collaborator, she said. Because in every room, she got the pictures of Hitler. And a lot of officers changed their clothing. They left their military belts, they left their hats, their uniforms. And they run away to the American zone. They'd rather be under the Americans than under the Russians. Because the Russians right away, boom, boom, shot them. The Americans are more liberal. They make first a trial and-- and they would get out a lot of them, from the Americans.

speaker: interviewee
text: So a lot of them run away. And they left behind evidence, their uniforms. So she said to me, she called me Rosal, with that German name, Rosal.

speaker: interviewee
text: I go, she says, in this wing. You go in that wing. You bring down whatever evidence you find. Because they're going to think we collaborators. She took me in to in that. That we are collaborators. And we're going to burn it. And this was my biggest satisfaction. Hitler's picture, all the uniforms, all the things. We made a big pile of it. And the [GERMAN], the commandant from that Jewish concentration camp run away on a motorcycle. A little doggy ran after him. But the doggy came after a half hour back. And he, I heard later, was caught by the Russians and shot, that commandant. And I wanted the boys to see that they'd be free soon. So I told her to move the whole pile next to the fence of the camp. I said-- I said maybe something can be caught on fire, let's move it away. Because I want them to see it. And they were standing and watch me-- she-- she put gas-- gasoline over the whole pile. And I lit the match. She gave me the matches. And I lit the match, and it started burning.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in that moment, I start crying. And she says to me, Rosal, what's the matter with you. Why are you crying?

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, don't worry. Our world will come back. She said to me. And the reason I was crying was that I am alone left in this world to see this burning. And I cannot share with nobody from my family that happy moment. And I started crying. This is the story of my escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I know that my immediate family perished in 1942. So I didn't have what to rush back to Warsaw. But I still want to go back, with the hopes that maybe I will find an uncle, a cousin, an aunt, maybe somebody. Maybe a close neighbor. And I went, practically by foot, a lot of miles. Because the way of the train-- the tracks were still broken from the war. And we went, a group of people, a group from that camp that they got free. There were Jews from-- from Sosnowiec-- Sosnowiec and Bedzin, not from Warsaw, these Jews. And they went to their towns, I went to Warsaw to look for relatives. Matter of fact, I met my husband there, in the Jewish community.

speaker: interviewer
text: Back in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm. And I-- First, I ask if somebody from the Blum family is alive. Nobody. I gave my address, where I'm living now. Because I figured in Warsaw, I cannot stay. Warsaw is for me like a reminder, a cemetery. I hate them. And I don't want to live in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I know I'm going to go back to Germany and the American zone. I'm going to try to come to the United States. So I went to sleep somewhere in Warsaw, in a little place, not in a suburb of Warsaw. We were sleeping-- I met over there my-- another friend from-- from concentration camp, another girl. And she said, you're lucky that you ran away because these five months that I was there was the worst five months.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans didn't give them nothing to eat. They starved. They died like flies. Epidemics and sicknesses, you wouldn't make it, she says, to me. This was-- you were very lucky that you ran away. You wouldn't make it. That's what she told me. And where we slept-- on straw, on the floor, we had to pay something. I think in German money we still paid her. A Polish woman gave us a place to sleep, the couple nights I was there. I said I have nothing to do no more in Warsaw. The town is all broken up. Everything they get. The Jewish section disappeared. Nothing was left of Warsaw at that time. And I went back to Germany to be in a DP camp for displaced persons. And over there, my son was born, in that camp. We got from the American Joint Distribution Centers-- you know, send us food, rations, clothing. And we waited for our next to come to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the DP camp have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bensheim. It was in Bensheim, a small town, not far from Frankfurt on Main. It was-- Bensheim, they had a lot of DP camps. They had Zeilsheim one, they had Eschwege, they had-- a lot-- they had a lot of DP camps. And we were managed by the Americans there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you met your husband there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This boy was born there. I got pregnant there with my daughter. But she was born here, after I was three months in this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to this-- how did you get to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to register as displaced people, as victims of Hitler, of the Nazis, Nazi victims. And we still had to wait our turn. This boy was undernourished, because I didn't have vitamins for him there. He had-- he was missing six pounds to his normal weight. I had to wait until I build him up.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was talking to the counselor, to the American counselor. If you let me stay longer, I might lose him all together. I won't have nobody to bring back-- to bring to America, because I don't have the right food for him here. I can't get it.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- the joint sent vitamins, things. But you don't like usually, there are people, crooks. They took it to sell it on the black market. And they told me that the vitamins, they're only giving to dying people. And he was almost like dying. But he still could stand. The doctor asked me, can he stand on his feet. If he can stand on his feet, we have to give it to children that they cannot no more stand on their feet, the vitamins. They took it for their business.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to this country? By boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: 10 Days by boat. And on these 10 days, I was already pregnant with my daughter. I was very sick. I thought I never going to make it. I said to my husband, they're going to bring me down and I'm-- and I'm nauseous. On-- how do you call it? They're going to have to take me down--

speaker: interviewer
text: On a stretcher.

speaker: interviewee
text: On stretcher, yeah. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had a hard time here, too. The first couple of years were very hard. My husband didn't know the language. I didn't know the language. I couldn't speak a word in English. We got an apartment that my uncle-- he was alive at that time-- helped me to get that apartment. I couldn't get on the-- being paid under the table. So the uncle gave me. They want $400 for an apartment full of roaches. Full of roaches. Dirty, filthy. Took me time until I could clean up. I couldn't clean up, because I was already pregnant. And my husband got a job right away as a presser. Because he didn't-- they took him in, and some friends that he-- his father's friends that they were here-- gave him a job in a factory as a presser. He never was a presser. He was a printer in Poland. But if you don't know the language, and there is a family with two kids to support-- so he became a presser. Worked very hard. He had to go for a major operation, because he had that bullet. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that's how it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you settle in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, on the-- first, yeah, the first, the HIAS. There was a Jewish organization for refugees, that they were waiting for us. They were waiting for us by the boat. And they took us to a dilapidated hotel, the HIAS, they call it. Somewhere on Lafayette Street, was the hotel. We were there 10-- 10 weeks, I was living there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they offered me apartments. And every apartment was on a fifth floor walk-up. And here I have two little kids. One, and one is going to be born soon. And how I going to walk up five floors?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I couldn't accept this. It took me 10-- 10 weeks, until somebody came and offered me an apartment in East New York. At that time, there was still a lot of Jews there. It was 1948. And East New York, on a second floor walk-up. But I had to give them-- $400 the people want. Just they don't want more than the tickets. They want to move over to California. Four people, two teenagers and the parents. I have to give them $100 a piece for the ticket to go to California. And I didn't have money. But my uncle helped me with this. So I got the apartment. And over there, I lived-- it was a cold apartment. The landlord didn't give us steam. We froze every winter. I had to dress the children to go to sleep in winter coats. That's how cold it was.
